reasoning: The Effective Federal Funds Rate (EFFR) is heavily managed by the Federal Reserve and currently prints flat at 4.33% every day, adhering closely to the 4.25-4.50% target range. Without an official rate hike, an increase is highly improbable. For the March 9 date, there is no scheduled FOMC meeting, rendering a target increase virtually impossible (probability floored at the 0.05 minimum). For upcoming dates (April 1, May 31), the market overwhelmingly prices in rate holds or cuts based on the FOMC's current stance, putting hike odds near zero. However, over the medium-to-long term (August 2025 to March 2026), uncertainty naturally climbs. Re-accelerating inflation from economic recalibrations, tariffs, or fiscal policy could force the Fed off its easing path and into unexpected hikes. Analysts assign a non-negligible tail risk (up to 40% across all of 2025 per some contrarian views) to policy tightening. Thus, estimates scale sequentially from the 0.05 lower bound up to 0.27, reflecting the strict accumulation of time horizon uncertainty.
